reviews.summary: The paper proposes precision-aware scaling laws that incorporate training and inference precision into the standard Chinchilla-style loss scaling framework. The central idea is an effective parameter count, Neff, that depends on bit precision of weights, activations, and attention as multiplicative factors, coupled with a data- and precision-dependent model of post-training quantization (PTQ) degradation. Using over 465 pretraining runs (3–16 bits, N up to 220M, D up to 26B) and additional PTQ experiments, the paper reports: (i) PTQ degradation increases with data (roughly with D/N), so overtraining can become harmful after quantization; (ii) during training, precision can be traded against parameters via Neff and the effects across model parts are approximately independent and multiplicative; and (iii) compute-optimal pretraining precision is around 7–8 bits when unconstrained, but increases with compute if model size is fixed.
strengths: The notion of a precision-dependent effective parameter count, Neff, which factorizes across weights/activations/attention as (1 − e−P/γ), is elegant and intuitive, yet specific enough to yield actionable predictions.

A unified functional form that ties together training-time precision effects and inference-time PTQ degradation via a single loss model is a valuable conceptual bridge between two subfields that are often treated separately.
The “robustification vs overtraining” decomposition for PTQ degradation is a clear, interpretable mechanism linking training precision to PTQ sensitivity.
Extensive empirical study: 465 pretraining runs across multiple precisions and components, plus multi-method PTQ evaluation, and R² values around 0.90–0.97 for key fits.
Validation of predictions on a separate set of compute-matched FP runs up to 1.7B parameters provides some external corroboration of the integer-type–fit conclusions.
Systematic sweeps that vary only one factor at a time (Pw, Pa, Pkv) and joint sweeps to test factorization assumptions.
The paper is generally clear and well-organized, with intuitive figures (e.g., iso-loss contours, predicted vs. actual plots) that support the proposed functional forms.
The decomposition of training and inference effects, and then their unification, is pedagogically effective.
Addresses a highly relevant and timely gap: existing scaling laws mostly ignore precision, while precision has become central to both cost and quality in large-scale training and inference.
Offers concrete guidance: implications for compute-optimal precision, the risks of overtraining when PTQ is required, and how to trade parameters against precision during training.
weaknesses: The Neff factorization and exponential forms are empirically driven; theoretical justification is limited and could be strengthened, especially for the multiplicative independence across model parts.
Training experiments quantize only the forward pass in many settings; compute optimality claims may depend on assuming backward pass speedups that were not realized in those experiments.

The cost model assumes compute scales linearly with precision; in practice, kernel overheads, memory bandwidth, and hardware-specific throughput nuances can make the realized speedups sublinear and nonuniform.
Primary experimental scale is modest (up to 220M parameters for most sweeps, 1.7B for a limited validation), which raises questions about extrapolation to 10B–400B regimes.

Architectural diversity is limited (one Transformer++ variant); many practical LLM training stacks include stabilization tricks or architectural changes for very low precision (e.g., FP8/FP4-friendly designs) that may alter the fitted constants or even the functional form.
PTQ analysis centers on weight-only quantization; inference regimes dominated by KV-cache bandwidth or activation quantization are common but not explored as end-state PTQ targets.

Some key implementation details (e.g., PTQ calibration data, per-channel vs per-tensor/group quant schemes, rounding modes) are deferred to appendices; more upfront clarity would help interpret generality.
The paper sometimes blurs “integer-type” vs “floating-point” precision fits and validations; clearer guidance on when to expect transferability is warranted.

While cited, a deeper empirical comparison to Dettmers & Zettlemoyer (k-bit inference scaling) or to recent robust PTQ methods (e.g., AWQ, QUIP) under the proposed scaling axes would strengthen the claims about PTQ degradation with overtraining.
Discussion of mixed-precision training literature (including backward pass quantization and FP8 pipelines) could be better connected to the assumptions underlying the cost model and the Neff mapping.
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive are the fitted γ parameters and CT, γD, γN, γpost to architecture changes (depth/width ratios, normalization variants) and to layerwise heterogeneity (e.g., per-layer precision or sensitivity)? Have you profiled per-layer deviations from the multiplicative Neff assumption?
2. Your training experiments often quantize only the forward pass. How do the main conclusions (e.g., compute-optimal precision, Neff mapping) change when both forward and backward passes use the same low precision and kernels, especially for FP8 pipelines that are increasingly common?
3. For PTQ, what calibration procedures and data sizes were used, and how does δPTQ depend on them? Do your conclusions about increasing δPTQ with D/N still hold under stronger PTQ methods (e.g., more calibration data, per-channel/group quantization, AWQ/QUIP variants)?
4. Can you provide sensitivity analyses for alternative cost models (e.g., memory-bound, communication-bound, mixed regimes) and show how P*(C) and the N vs D allocation change as these assumptions vary?
5. Do your conclusions extend to inference-time quantization of activations or KV-cache directly (beyond weight-only PTQ)? Given many inference workloads are KV-bound, can your unified scaling law be adapted to predict end-to-end degradation in such scenarios?
6. How stable are the fits across different datasets or data mixtures (e.g., web vs. curated corpora)? Do you expect γD ≈ γN to persist across these settings?
reviews.assessment: This is a timely and well-executed paper tackling an important omission in current scaling-law practice: the role of precision during both training and inference. The proposal of a precision-aware Neff and a unified δPTQ model is conceptually clean, empirically supported with a substantial number of runs, and yields concrete, actionable implications (e.g., rising PTQ fragility with overtraining, compute-optimal precision near 7–8 bits in unconstrained settings, and a logarithmic dependence of optimal precision on compute when N is fixed). The main reservations are about external validity at frontier scales, the reliance on forward-only quantization and a linear-in-P cost model, and limited architectural/dataset diversity. Nonetheless, the work advances the state of understanding and provides a solid foundation for further validation and theory. I recommend acceptance, contingent on strengthening the caveats, clarifying implementation specifics, and, if possible, adding a brief sensitivity analysis to alternate cost/precision-throughput assumptions.
